relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The Eternal visited Sarah and did exactly as promised.
At the appointed time, Sarah conceived and bore the child of promise.
Abraham named the child Isaac, laughter made flesh.
Isaac entered the covenant sign on the eighth day.
Abraham was one hundred when the impossible child arrived.
Sarah’s laughter turned from hidden disbelief into shared joy.
She marveled that she, Sarah, had nursed a child in Abraham’s old age.
Isaac grew, and a feast marked his weaning.
Sarah saw Hagar’s son laughing, and the laughter of the household became conflict.
Sarah demanded separation, refusing inheritance to the son of the slave-woman beside Isaac.
Abraham was deeply grieved because Ishmael was his son.
The Divine distinguished Isaac’s covenant line while still holding Ishmael inside promise.
Ishmael too would become a nation, because he was Abraham’s seed.
Hagar and Ishmael were sent into the wilderness with bread and water, carrying both rejection and promise.
When the water was gone, Hagar placed the boy beneath the shrubs.
She sat at a distance and wept, unable to bear what she feared was coming.
The Divine heard the boy, and the messenger called Hagar by name: Do not fear.
She was told to lift him up and hold him, for he would become a great nation.
Her eyes were opened, and she saw the well that would preserve life.
The Divine was with Ishmael as he grew in the wilderness.
He dwelled in Paran, and Hagar secured a future for him from Egypt.
Abimelech recognized that the Divine was with Abraham in all he did.
He asked Abraham for an oath of honest dealing and covenant-kindness in the land of sojourning.
Abraham agreed to swear.
But Abraham also confronted the injustice over a seized well.
Abimelech denied knowing who had done it.
The two men cut covenant.
Seven ewe-lambs were set apart as a witness.
Abimelech asked what the seven lambs meant.
They witnessed that Abraham had dug the well.
The place was called Beersheba, well of oath and seven.
The covenant was sealed, and Abimelech returned to the Philistine land.
Abraham planted a tamarisk and called on the Eternal as El Olam, God of the Age and Forever.
He remained a sojourner in the land many days.
